document: data (use and access) act 2025 (United Kingdom - Row 5)

sections.heading: Customer data and business data
sections.title: Definitions for customer and business data in data processing
sections.summary: This section defines key terms including 'business data' (information about goods/services/digital content supplied by a trader), 'customer data' (information about customers), 'data holder' (trader or processor), and 'data regulations'. It specifies conditions for a person being a customer of a trader, including purchasing goods/services or receiving content free of charge. The definitions establish the scope of data covered under the regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines what constitutes customer and business data for regulatory purposes, which is critical for determining data transfer scope in cross-border contexts.

requirements.text: A person is a customer of a trader if they purchased goods/services/digital content from the trader
A person is a customer of a trader if they received goods/services/digital content free of charge from the trader

sections.source_quote: Customer data and business data (1) This Part confers powers on the Secretary of State and the Treasury to make provision in connection with access to customer data and business data.

sections.heading: Power to make provision in connection with customer data
sections.title: Regulations for customer data access and sharing
sections.summary: This section grants the Secretary of State or Treasury power to create regulations requiring data holders to provide customer data to customers or authorized persons. It defines 'third party recipient' and allows regulations to mandate data production, collection, retention, and rectification of inaccurate customer data. The regulations must consider impacts on customers, data holders, small businesses, innovation, and competition.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for regulatory frameworks that govern how customer data is shared with authorized third parties, which impacts cross-border data transfers and data sharing mechanisms.

requirements.text: Data holder must provide customer data to customer at customer's request
Data holder must provide customer data to authorized person at customer's request or authorized person's request
Data holder must produce, collect or retain customer data at customer's request
Data holder must rectify inaccurate customer data at customer or authorized person's request

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State or the Treasury may by regulations make provision requiring a data holder to provide customer data—(a) to the customer, at the customer's request, or (b) to a person of a specified description who is authorised by the customer to receive the data (an "authorised person"), at the customer's request or at the authorized person's request.

sections.heading: Customer data: supplementary
sections.title: Regulatory provisions for customer data management
sections.summary: This section outlines potential regulatory provisions for customer data management, including procedures for customer authorization, restrictions on authorized persons, record-keeping requirements, and dispute resolution mechanisms. Regulations may require data holders to provide customer data on specific occasions, for specified periods, or at intervals, and may mandate the use of specified facilities or services like dashboards and APIs.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies regulatory mechanisms for customer data sharing, including requirements for data holders to provide customer data on specific occasions and use specified facilities or services, which affects cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Regulations may require data holder to provide customer data on one or more occasions, for a specified period or at specified intervals
Regulations may require data holder, customer or third party recipient to use specified facilities or services, including dashboard services
Regulations may require data holder or third party recipient to comply with specified standards
Regulations may require data holder or third party recipient to provide specified assistance in connection with facilities or services

sections.source_quote: The regulations may include—(a) provision about the procedure by which customers authorise persons to receive customer data or to do other things

sections.heading: Power to make provision in connection with business data
sections.title: Regulations for business data access and sharing
sections.summary: This section grants the Secretary of State or Treasury power to create regulations requiring data holders to publish or provide business data to customers or specified persons. It defines 'third party recipient' for business data and allows regulations to mandate data production, collection, retention, and publication of business data. The regulations must consider impacts on customers, data holders, small businesses, innovation, and competition.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows for regulatory frameworks that govern how business data is shared with customers or third parties, which impacts cross-border data transfers and data sharing mechanisms.

requirements.text: Data holder must publish or provide business data to customer of the trader
Data holder must publish or provide business data to another person of a specified description
Data holder must produce, collect or retain business data

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State or the Treasury may by regulations make provision requiring a data holder to publish business data or to provide business data—(a) to a customer of the trader to whom the business data relates, or (b) to another person of a specified description.

sections.heading: Business data: supplementary
sections.title: Regulatory provisions for business data management
sections.summary: This section outlines potential regulatory provisions for business data management, including requirements for business data to be provided on request, restrictions on approved recipients, record-keeping requirements, and dispute resolution mechanisms. Regulations may require data holders to provide business data on specific occasions, for specified periods, or at intervals, and may mandate the use of specified facilities or services like dashboards and APIs.
sections.dtia_summary: Specifies regulatory mechanisms for business data sharing, including requirements for data holders to provide business data on specific occasions and use specified facilities or services, which affects cross-border data transfers.

requirements.text: Regulations may require business data to be provided on request
Regulations may require data holder to provide business data on one or more occasions, for a specified period or at specified intervals
Regulations may require data holder, customer or third party recipient to use specified facilities or services
Regulations may require data holder or third party recipient to comply with specified standards

sections.source_quote: The regulations may require business data to be provided on request and make provision about requests, including provision for requests to be made by a customer, a third party recipient or another person

sections.heading: I9
sections.title: Restrictions on powers of investigation etc
sections.summary: This section restricts the powers of investigation under this Part, prohibiting entry to private dwellings without a warrant and limiting the types of information that can be required from individuals. It also specifies exceptions for legal advice, communications related to proceedings, and evidence of offences.
sections.dtia_summary: Restrictions on investigation powers could impact data transfer impact assessments by limiting the scope of data that can be collected across borders for enforcement purposes.

requirements.text: Regulations under this Part may not authorise entry to a private dwelling without a warrant issued by a justice
Regulations under this Part may not require a person to provide information within subsections (2) to (7) to a decision-maker, an interface body or an enforcer
Information is within this subsection if requiring a person to provide the information would involve an infringement of the privileges of either House of Parliament
Information is within this subsection if requiring a person to provide the information relates to communications made in the course of proceedings
Information is within this subsection if requiring a person to provide the information relates to evidence of offences

An oral or written statement provided by a person in response to a request for information may not be used in evidence against that person on a prosecution for an offence unless in the proceedings the person provides information inconsistent with the statement

An oral or written statement provided by a person in response to a request for information may not be used in evidence against that person on a prosecution for an offence unless evidence relating to the statement is adduced or a question relating to it is asked by that person or on that person's behalf

sections.source_quote: Regulations under this Part may not authorise entry to a private dwelling without a warrant issued by a justice

sections.heading: Financial penalties
sections.title: Financial penalties for non-compliance
sections.summary: This section mandates that regulations must specify the amount of financial penalties, require enforcers to produce guidance on penalty determination, publish guidance, and provide written notices to affected persons before imposing penalties. It also includes provisions for appeals and penalty adjustments.
sections.dtia_summary: Financial penalties could impact data transfer impact assessments by introducing financial consequences for non-compliance with cross-border data handling regulations.

requirements.text: The regulations must provide for the amount of a financial penalty to be a specified amount or an amount determined in accordance with the
The regulations must include provision requiring an enforcer to produce guidance about how the enforcer proposes to exercise any discretion to determine the amount of a financial penalty
The regulations must include provision requiring an enforcer to publish the guidance
The regulations must include provision requiring an enforcer to give written notice of the proposed financial penalty
The regulations must include provision ensuring that the person is given an opportunity to make representations about the proposed financial penalty
The regulations must include provision requiring the enforcer to decide whether to impose the financial penalty after the period for making representations
The regulations must include provision requiring the enforcer to give written notice imposing the penalty if they decide to impose it
The regulations must include provision enabling a person on whom a financial penalty is imposed to appeal to a court or tribunal

sections.source_quote: The regulations must provide for the amount of a financial penalty to be— a specified amount or an amount determined in accordance with the

sections.heading: Fees
sections.title: Fees for data handling activities
sections.summary: This section allows the Secretary of State or Treasury to set fees for data holders, decision-makers, interface bodies, and enforcers who perform duties or exercise powers under regulations. Fees may be specified amounts, determined based on costs, or subject to increases over time, with requirements for transparency and recovery.
sections.dtia_summary: Fees for data handling activities could impact data transfer impact assessments by introducing financial mechanisms for cross-border data processing.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State or the Treasury may make provision enabling a person listed in subsection (2) to require other persons to pay fees in connection with activities described in subsection (3)
The Secretary of State or the Treasury may make provision about what must or may be done with amounts paid as fees
Regulations may only provide for a fee to be payable by persons that appear to the Secretary of State or Treasury to be capable of being directly affected by the performance of duties
Regulations may provide for the amount of a fee to be an amount which is intended to exceed the cost of the things in connection with which the fee is charged
Regulations must provide for the amount of a fee to be a specified amount or an amount determined in accordance with the
Regulations may provide for the amount, or maximum amount, of a fee to increase at specified times and by a specified amount or an amount determined in accordance with the
Regulations enabling a person to determine the amount of a fee must require the person to publish information about the amount and how it is determined

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State or the Treasury may by regulations make provision enabling a person listed in subsection (2), or a person acting on their behalf, to require other persons to pay fees in connection with activities described in subsection (3)

sections.title: FCA coordination with other regulators
sections.summary: This section allows the Treasury to amend the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 to coordinate the FCA's functions with other regulators. It enables the addition or removal of functions from the FCA's definition of 'relevant functions' and objectives related to those functions.
sections.dtia_summary: FCA coordination with other regulators affects how data regulations are implemented across financial services, requiring assessment of regulatory alignment in data transfer impact assessments.

requirements.text: The Treasury may amend the definition of 'relevant functions' in the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 to add or remove FCA functions
The Treasury may amend the definition of 'objectives' in the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2: 2013 to add or remove FCA objectives

sections.source_quote: The Treasury may by regulations amend section 98 of the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 (payment systems: duty of the FCA and other regulators to ensure co-ordinated exercise of relevant functions) by—(a) amending the definition of "relevant functions" so as to add or remove a function conferred on the FCA by regulations under this Part, and (b) amending the definition of "objectives" so as to add or remove an objective of the FCA relevant to such a function.

sections.heading: nothing in this section, or in regulations under subsection (1) or (9), prevents the (cont.)
sections.title: FCA interface requirements for financial services
sections.summary: This section establishes requirements for the FCA to implement interface standards for financial services providers to share customer and business data. It mandates that financial services providers use prescribed interfaces, comply with interface standards, and participate in interface arrangements when exchanging data. The FCA may impose additional requirements for non-compliance, including conduct reviews and remedial actions.
sections.dtia_summary: FCA interface requirements create specific data exchange protocols that must be assessed in data transfer impact assessments to ensure compliance with financial services data sharing standards.

requirements.text: Financial services providers must use prescribed interfaces when providing or receiving customer data
Persons linked to financial services must comply with interface standards when receiving data from financial services providers
Section 2(4) actors must use interfaces when taking relevant financial services actions
The FCA may impose requirements to review a person's conduct
The FCA may impose requirements for remedial actions
The FCA may impose requirements for redress for loss or damage

sections.source_quote: The Treasury may by regulations make provision enabling or requiring the Financial Conduct Authority (referred to in this Part as "the FCA") to make rules—(a) requiring financial services providers described in the regulations to use a prescribed interface, comply with prescribed interface standards or participate in prescribed interface arrangements, when providing or receiving customer data or business data which is required to be provided by or to the financial services provider by data regulations

sections.heading: The FCA and co-ordination with other regulators
sections.title: FCA coordination with other regulators
sections.summary: This section allows the Treasury to amend the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 to coordinate the FCA's functions with other regulators. It enables the addition or removal of functions from the FCA's definition of 'relevant functions' and objectives related to those functions.
sections.dtia_summary: FCA coordination with other regulators affects how data regulations are implemented across financial services, requiring assessment of regulatory alignment in data transfer impact assessments.

requirements.text: The Treasury may amend the definition of 'relevant functions' in the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 to add or remove FCA functions
The Treasury may amend the definition of 'objectives' in the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2: 2013 to add or remove FCA objectives

sections.source_quote: The Treasury may by regulations amend section 98 of the Financial Services (Banking Reform) Act 2013 (payment systems: duty of the FCA and other regulators to ensure co-ordinated exercise of relevant functions) by—(a) amending the definition of "relevant functions" so as to add or remove a function conferred on the FCA by regulations under this Part, and (b) amending the definition of "objectives" so as to add or remove an objective of the FCA relevant to such a function.

sections.heading: Liability in damages
sections.title: Liability for regulatory actions
sections.summary: This section provides that persons listed as public authorities or their representatives are not liable for damages for actions taken in exercising functions under data regulations. The regulations may not remove liability for acts done in bad faith or prevent awards of damages for unlawful acts under the Human Rights Act 1998.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulatory immunity for public authorities in data compliance actions affects liability assessments in data transfer impact studies.

requirements.text: Public authorities and their representatives are not liable for damages for actions taken under data regulations
Regulations may not remove liability for acts done in bad faith
Regulations may not prevent awards of damages for unlawful acts under the Human Rights Act 1998

sections.source_quote: (1) The Secretary of State or the Treasury may by regulations provide that a person listed in subsection (2) is not liable in damages for anything done or omitted to be done in the exercise of functions conferred by or under regulations made under this Part.

sections.heading: Duty to review regulations
sections.title: Regulatory review requirements
sections.summary: This section requires the Secretary of State or Treasury to review and publish reports on the appropriateness of data regulations. The reviews must assess whether regulations remain appropriate considering their intended objectives and the matters considered during implementation.
sections.dtia_summary: Regulatory review cycles for data provisions must be assessed in data transfer impact studies to ensure ongoing compliance with evolving regulatory needs.

requirements.text: The relevant person must review Part 1 provisions made by them under data regulations
The relevant person must prepare and publish a report on each review
The relevant person must lay a copy of the report before Parliament
The first report must be published within 5 years of regulation implementation
Subsequent reports must be published at intervals of not more than 5 years

sections.source_quote: (1) The relevant person must, by regulations, provide for the review of provision made by the relevant person in exercise of powers to make regulations under other sections in this Part ("Part 1 provision")

sections.heading: Restrictions on processing and data protection
sections.title: Restrictions on processing and data protection
sections.summary: This section prohibits processing of information that breaches confidentiality obligations or other restrictions on processing. It explicitly states that regulations under this Part may not authorize processing that contravenes data protection legislation, with the caveat that the determination of contravention must consider the specific powers or duties imposed by the regulations in question.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes that cross-border data transfers must not violate confidentiality obligations or other processing restrictions, requiring DTIAs to assess whether transfers comply with data protection laws and confidentiality duties.

requirements.text: Regulations under this Part may not authorize processing that breaches confidentiality obligations
Regulations under this Part may not authorize processing that contravenes data protection legislation

sections.source_quote: Regulations under this Part may provide for the processing of information in accordance with the regulations not to be in breach of— (a) any obligation of confidence owed by the person processing the information, or (b) any other restriction on the processing of information (however imposed).

sections.heading: Regulations under this Part: supplementary
sections.title: Regulations under this Part: supplementary provisions
sections.summary: This section outlines the scope and limitations of regulations made under this Part, including their authority to make general provisions, differentiate by purpose or area, specify forms and procedures, set timeframes, reference standards, confer functions, and make consequential provisions. It also restricts regulations from setting maximum fines or penalty amounts without specific exceptions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines the flexibility and constraints on regulatory provisions that could impact data transfers, particularly regarding the scope of regulations that might govern cross-border data flows.

requirements.text: Regulations may make provision generally or in relation to particular cases
Regulations may make different provision for different purposes or areas
Regulations may make provision about the form and manner in which things must or may be done
Regulations may make provision about the content of requests, notices or other documents
Regulations may make provision about the time by which, or period within which, things must or may be done
Regulations may make provision by reference to standards, arrangements, specifications or technical requirements as published from time to time
Regulations may confer functions on a person, including functions involving the exercise of a discretion, and make provision in connection with the procedure for exercising the functions

Regulations may not require or enable a person to set the maximum amount of a fine for an offence
Regulations may not require or enable a person to set the amount or maximum amount of, or of an increase in, a penalty or fee

sections.source_quote: Regulations under this Part may (among other things)— (a) make provision generally or in relation to particular cases; (b) make different provision for different purposes or areas; (c) make provision about the form and manner in which things must or may be done; (d) make provision about the content of requests, notices or other documents; (e) make provision about the time by which, or period within which, things must or may be done; (f) make provision by reference to standards, arrangements, specifications or technical requirements as published from time to time; (g) confer functions on a person, including functions involving the exercise of a discretion, and make provision in connection with the procedure for exercising the functions; (h) make consequential, supplementary, incidental, transitional, transitory or saving provision.

sections.heading: I42
sections.title: Introductory to digital verification services
sections.summary: This section introduces the framework for securing digital verification services, including the trust framework, supplementary codes, register, information gateway, and trust mark.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes the foundational elements for digital verification services, which are critical for ensuring reliable data transfers in digital environments.

requirements.text: Digital verification services must be secured by a trust framework, supplementary codes, register, information gateway, and trust mark

sections.source_quote: This Part contains provision to secure the reliability of digital verification services by means of— (a) a trust framework (see section 28), (b) supplementary codes (see section 29), (c) a register (see section 32), (d) an information gateway (see section 45), and (e) a trust mark (see section 50).

sections.heading: "specified" means specified, or of a description specified, by regulations
sections.title: Supplementary codes
sections.summary: This section allows the Secretary of State to prepare and publish supplementary codes that supplement the DVS trust framework, including consultation requirements, the ability to revise codes, and specifications for when codes come into force.
sections.dtia_summary: This section provides supplementary rules for digital verification services that can be used to enhance the trust framework, critical for ensuring reliable data transfers.

requirements.text: Secretary of State may prepare and publish supplementary codes
Supplementary codes may include rules relating to the conduct of persons providing services
Secretary of State must consult with affected parties before publishing supplementary codes
Secretary of State may revise and republish supplementary codes
Supplementary codes must specify the time they come into force (not earlier than publication time)
Supplementary codes may set different rules for different digital verification services
Supplementary codes may specify that provisions come into force at different times for different purposes
Supplementary codes may make transitional or saving provision
Supplementary codes may provide that pre-revision certificates are ignored from a specified date

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State may prepare and publish one or more sets of rules concerning the provision of digital verification services which supplement the DVS trust framework.

sections.heading: PART 2
sections.title: Review of DVS trust framework and supplementary codes
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Secretary of State review the DVS trust framework and each supplementary code (not withdrawn) at least every 12 months, with consultation requirements for these reviews.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures ongoing evaluation of the trust framework and supplementary codes, critical for maintaining the reliability of digital verification services.

requirements.text: Secretary of State must review DVS trust framework at least every 12 months
Secretary of State must review each supplementary code (not withdrawn) at least every 12 months
Secretary of State must consult with affected parties before conducting reviews

sections.source_quote: At least every 12 months, the Secretary of State must— (a) carry out a review of the DVS trust framework, and (b) at the same time, carry out a review of each supplementary code which has not been withdrawn.

sections.heading: Review of DVS trust framework and supplementary codes
sections.title: Review of DVS trust framework and supplementary codes
sections.summary: This section mandates that the Secretary of State review the DVS trust framework and each supplementary code (not withdrawn) at least every 12 months, with consultation requirements for these reviews.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures ongoing evaluation of the trust framework and supplementary codes, critical for maintaining the reliability of digital verification services.

requirements.text: Secretary of State must review DVS trust framework at least every 12 months
Secretary of State must review each supplementary code (not withdrawn) at least every 12 months
Secretary of State must consult with affected parties before conducting reviews

sections.source_quote: At least every 12 months, the Secretary of State must— (a) carry out a review of the DVS trust framework, and (b) at the same time, carry out a review of each supplementary code which has not been withdrawn.

sections.heading: DVS register
sections.title: Public availability of digital verification service register
sections.summary: The Secretary of State must establish and maintain a register of entities providing digital verification services. The register is publicly accessible to ensure transparency. This requirement applies to the Secretary of State and the entities subject to registration under this Part.
sections.dtia_summary: Public availability of the DVS register ensures transparency for data transfer assessments involving digital verification services.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State must establish and maintain a register of persons providing digital verification services
The register is referred to in this Part as the DVS register
The Secretary of State must make the DVS register publicly available

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State must establish and maintain a register of persons providing digital verification services. The register is referred to in this Part as the DVS register. The Secretary of State must make the DVS register publicly available.

sections.heading: Registration in the DVS register
sections.title: Registration requirements for digital verification services
sections.summary: The Secretary of State must register entities providing digital verification services if they certify compliance with the DVS trust framework, apply for registration, and meet payment conditions. Registration is subject to the Secretary of State's power to refuse registration under section 34 and duties to refuse registration under sections 34(10) and 41(10).
sections.dtia_summary: Registration of digital verification services requires compliance with the DVS trust framework, which impacts data transfer assessments involving digital verification services.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State must register a person providing digital verification services in the DVS register if the person certifies digital verification services are provided in accordance with the DVS trust framework
The person applies to be registered in the DVS register in respect of one or more digital verification services
The person pays the required fee
The Secretary of State may not register a person if the conditions in subsection (1) are not met
The register must record digital verification services for which a person is registered

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State must register a person providing digital verification services in the DVS register if— (a) certifying that digital verification services provided by the person are provided in accordance with the DVS trust framework, (b) the person applies to be registered in the DVS register in respect of one or more of the digital verification services to which the certificate relates, (c) the person pays the required fee.

sections.heading: Power to refuse registration in the DVS register
sections.title: Power to refuse registration in DVS register
sections.summary: The Secretary of State may refuse registration of digital verification service providers if they fail to comply with the DVS trust framework or national security concerns are at stake. The refusal process requires written notice, a 21-day period for written representations, and consideration of oral representations if applicable.
sections.dtia_summary: The power to refuse registration based on compliance with the DVS trust framework impacts data transfer assessments involving digital verification services.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State may refuse to register a person providing digital verification services in the DVS register if the Secretary of State considers it necessary for national security
The Secretary of State may refuse to register a person if satisfied the person is failing to comply with the DVS trust framework
The Secretary of State must inform the person in writing before refusing registration
The notice must state the person's name and address
The notice must state the reason for refusal (national security or non-compliance)
The notice must specify whether a period is intended and, if so, what period
The notice must state that the person may make written representations
The person may make written representations within 21 days
The Secretary of State must consider any written or oral representations made by the person
The Secretary of State must inform the person in writing if registration is refused

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State may refuse to register a person providing digital verification services in the DVS register if the Secretary of State— (a) considers that it is necessary to do so in the interests of national security, or (b) is satisfied that the person is failing to comply with the DVS trust framework in respect of one or more of the digital verification services in respect of which the person applies to be registered.

sections.heading: Registration of additional services
sections.title: Amending DVS register for additional services
sections.summary: The Secretary of State must amend the DVS register to record additional digital verification services provided by a registered entity if the entity certifies compliance with the DVS trust framework and meets payment conditions. The amendment is conditional on meeting the specified criteria.
sections.dtia_summary: Amending the DVS register for additional services requires compliance with the DVS trust framework, which affects data transfer assessments involving digital verification services.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State must amend the DVS register to record that the person is registered in respect of additional digital verification services
The person certifies digital verification services are provided in accordance with the DVS trust framework
The person applies for their entry in the register to be amended
The person pays the required fee

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State must amend the DVS register to record that the person is also registered in respect of the additional services referred to in subsection (1).

sections.heading: Supplementary notes
sections.title: Supplementary notes in DVS register
sections.summary: The Secretary of State must include supplementary notes in the DVS register for digital verification services that comply with supplementary codes, provided the entity holds a certificate from an accredited conformity assessment body and meets payment conditions. The notes are subject to withdrawal if the supplementary code is withdrawn.
sections.dtia_summary: Supplementary notes in the DVS register provide additional information on digital verification services, which can impact data transfer assessments.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State must include a note in the DVS register recording that the person provides digital verification services in accordance with a supplementary code
The person holds a certificate from an accredited conformity assessment body
The person applies for a note about one or more services to be included in the register
The person pays the required fee

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State must include a note in the entry relating to the person in the DVS register recording that the person provides, in accordance with the supplementary code referred to in subsection (1), the services in respect of which the person made the application referred to in that subsection.

sections.heading: Addition of services to supplementary notes
sections.title: Amending supplementary notes for additional services
sections.summary: The Secretary of State must amend supplementary notes in the DVS register to record additional digital verification services provided by a person in accordance with the supplementary code, provided the entity meets payment conditions and certification requirements. The amendment is conditional on the supplementary code not being withdrawn.
sections.dtia_summary: Amending supplementary notes for additional services requires compliance with the supplementary code, which impacts data transfer assessments involving digital verification services.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State must amend the note to record that the person provides additional services in accordance with the supplementary code
The person certifies digital verification services are provided in accordance with the supplementary code
The person applies for the note to be amended
The person pays the required fee

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State must amend the note to record that the person also provides the additional services referred to in subsection (1) in accordance with the supplementary code to which the note relates.

sections.heading: Applications for registration, supplementary notes, etc
sections.title: Registration application form specifications
sections.summary: The Secretary of State can determine the format, required information, documents, and submission method for registration applications under sections 33, 35, 36, and 37. These determinations may vary by purpose and must be published. The Secretary of State may also revise these determinations and must publish any revisions.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State may determine the form of an application under section 33, 35, 36 or 37
The Secretary of State may determine the information to be contained in or provided with the application
The Secretary of State may determine the documents to be provided with the application
The Secretary of State may determine the manner in which the application is to be submitted
A determination may make different provision for different purposes
The Secretary of State must publish a determination
The Secretary of State may revise a determination
If the Secretary of State revises a determination the Secretary of State must publish the determination as revised

sections.source_quote: 38 (1) The Secretary of State may determine— (a) the form of an application under section 33, 35, 36 or 37, (b) the information to be contained in or provided with the application, (c) the documents to be provided with the application, and (d) the manner in which the application is to be submitted.

sections.heading: Fees for applications for registration, supplementary notes, etc
sections.title: Registration application fees regulations
sections.summary: The Secretary of State can make regulations for fees related to registration applications under sections 33, 35, 36, and 37, and for continued registration in the DVS register. Fees must be specified in amount or maximum amount, may exceed administrative costs, and can include payment timing, methods, refunds, interest, and recovery as civil debt. Regulations must follow negative resolution procedure.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State may by regulations make provision for or in connection with the payment of fees for applications under sections 33, 35, 36 and 37
The Secretary of State may by regulations make provision for or in connection with the payment of fees in connection with continued registration in the DVS register

This section also removes specific fee-related clauses from the Commissioner's authority.

This section does not specify the content of the analysis but mandates its annual publication.

sections.source_quote: Storing information in the terminal equipment of a subscriber or user (1) Subject to Schedule A1, a person must not store information, or gain access to information stored, in the terminal equipment of a subscriber or user.

sections.heading: Emergency alerts: interpretation of time periods
sections.title: Emergency alert time period calculation
sections.summary: This section modifies the time period for emergency alerts to begin counting from the day after the relevant public authority's time period expires, ensuring alignment with public authority notifications. The amendment applies to the PEC Regulations governing emergency alerts and clarifies the calculation of the 7-day period for emergency alert responses.
sections.dtia_summary: Defines when the 7-day emergency alert period starts—after the public authority's time period expires.

requirements.text: 7-day emergency alert period begins after public authority's time period expires

sections.source_quote: In regulation 16A of the PEC Regulations (emergency alerts), in paragraph (6), for the words from "7 days" to "paragraph (3)(b)" substitute "the period of 7 days beginning with the day on which the time period specified by the relevant public authority pursuant to paragraph (3)(b) expires"

sections.heading: Use of electronic mail for direct marketing by charities
sections.title: Charity email marketing with consent
sections.summary: This section allows charities to send direct marketing emails if they obtained contact details through recipient interest or support for charitable purposes, provided they offer a simple refusal mechanism at collection and subsequent communications. The regulation defines 'charity' and 'charitable purpose' to align with relevant legislation and ensures compliance with data protection principles for charitable communications.
sections.dtia_summary: Permits charities to send marketing emails with specific consent conditions; requires clear refusal mechanism at collection and each communication.

requirements.text: Charity may send emails for direct marketing if sole purpose is charitable purposes
Charity must obtain contact details through recipient interest or support
Recipient must have simple refusal mechanism at collection and each communication

sections.source_quote: A charity may send or instigate the sending of electronic mail for the purposes of direct marketing where—(a) the sole purpose of the direct marketing is to further one or more of the charity's charitable purposes

sections.heading: Commissioner's enforcement powers
sections.title: Information Commissioner's enforcement powers
sections.summary: This section amends the Information Commissioner's enforcement powers by removing specific regulations related to security audits and personal data breach penalties, while adding provisions for Northern Ireland penalties and requiring parliamentary consultation before statutory instrument amendments. The changes focus on streamlining enforcement mechanisms and ensuring compliance with data protection regulations.
sections.dtia_summary: Modifies enforcement powers by removing audit requirements and breach penalty regulations; adds Northern Ireland penalty provisions; requires parliamentary consultation for statutory instruments.

requirements.text: Omit regulation 5B (personal data breach: audit)
Omit regulation 31A (third party information notices)
Omit regulation 31B (appeals against third party information notices)
Secretary of State may amend penalties via statutory instruments
Statutory instruments require parliamentary consultation

sections.source_quote: Commissioner's enforcement powers (1) The PEC Regulations are amended in accordance with subsections (2) to (8).

sections.heading: Codes of conduct
sections.title: Codes of conduct for data protection compliance
sections.summary: This section establishes a framework for representative bodies to develop and submit codes of conduct for compliance with data protection regulations. It requires the Commissioner to review, approve, and publish codes that include monitoring mechanisms and conflict-of-interest safeguards. The codes must address regulatory obligations, dispute resolution, and compliance monitoring, with accreditation for monitoring bodies.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires codes of conduct for data protection compliance; mandates monitoring mechanisms; establishes accreditation for compliance monitoring bodies.

requirements.text: Commissioner must encourage representative bodies to produce codes of conduct
Codes must include monitoring mechanisms for compliance
Monitoring bodies must be accredited by Commissioner
Accredited bodies must handle complaints and publish procedures

sections.source_quote: 32A. Codes of conduct (1) The Commissioner must encourage representative bodies to produce codes of conduct intended to contribute to compliance with these Regulations.

sections.heading: The Information Commission
sections.title: Establishment of Information Commission
sections.summary: This section amends the Data Protection Act 2018 by establishing a new body called the Information Commission. It inserts a definition for 'The Commission' and updates references to the Information Commissioner in relevant sections and schedules. The changes include creating a new section (114A) that formally establishes the Commission and modifying the index of defined expressions to include it.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a new regulatory body for data protection, which may affect how data transfers are monitored and enforced across borders by replacing the Information Commissioner with the Information Commission.

requirements.text: A body corporate called the Information Commission is established.
Schedule 12A makes further provision about the Commission.
The Commission means the Information Commission (see section 114A).

sections.source_quote: The Information Commission (1) A body corporate called the Information Commission is established.

sections.heading: Abolition of the office of Information Commissioner
sections.title: Abolition of Information Commissioner Office
sections.summary: This section abolishes the Information Commissioner office and amends the Data Protection Act 2018 to remove all references to the Information Commissioner. It deletes the relevant section (114) and updates the index of defined expressions to exclude the Commissioner, ensuring all references to the office are treated as references to the Information Commission.
sections.dtia_summary: This section removes the Information Commissioner as a regulatory body, shifting oversight to the Information Commission, which may impact data transfer compliance and enforcement mechanisms.

requirements.text: The office of Information Commissioner is abolished.
The Data Protection Act 2018 is amended as follows.
Omit section 114 (the Information Commissioner).
Omit the entry for the Commissioner in section 206.

sections.source_quote: The office of Information Commissioner is abolished.

sections.heading: Transfer of functions to the Information Commission
sections.title: Transfer of Information Commissioner Functions
sections.summary: This section transfers the functions of the Information Commissioner to the Information Commission. It ensures that all references to the Information Commissioner in enactments, legislation, and documents are treated as references to the Information Commission, including provisions for cross-jurisdictional applicability across the UK.
sections.dtia_summary: This section ensures continuity of data protection oversight by transferring the Information Commissioner's functions to the Information Commission, which may affect how data transfers are regulated across different UK jurisdictions.

requirements.text: The functions of the Information Commissioner are transferred to the Information Commission.
References to the Information Commissioner are treated as references to the Information Commission.
Enactment includes subordinate legislation, measures, and instruments from various UK jurisdictions.

sections.source_quote: The functions of the Information Commissioner are transferred to the Information Commission.

sections.heading: Transfer of property etc to the Information Commission
sections.title: Transfer of Property to Information Commission
sections.summary: This section allows the Secretary of State to create a transfer scheme for moving property, rights, and liabilities from the Information Commissioner to the Information Commission. It specifies what can be transferred, including post-scheme acquisitions, and permits provisions for continuity of legal proceedings and references to the Information Commissioner.
sections.dtia_summary: This section facilitates the transfer of the Information Commissioner's assets and responsibilities to the Information Commission, which may impact data transfer compliance and enforcement mechanisms.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State may make a scheme for the transfer of property, rights and liabilities from the Information Commissioner to the Information Commission.
Property acquired after the scheme comes into effect may be transferred.
A transfer scheme may make provision about the continuing effect of things done by the Information Commissioner.
A transfer scheme may make provision for references to the Information Commissioner to be treated as references to the Information Commission.

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State may make a scheme for the transfer of property, rights and liabilities from the Information Commissioner to the Information Commission.

sections.heading: Information standards for health and adult social care in England
sections.title: Health and Social Care Information Standards
sections.summary: This section refers to Schedule 15, which establishes information standards for health and adult social care in England under the Health and Social Care Act 2012. It covers information technology standards for these sectors, but does not directly address data transfers or processing.
sections.dtia_summary: This section sets information standards for health and social care in England, which may indirectly impact data transfers involving health-related personal information.

requirements.text: Schedule 15 makes provision about information standards for health and adult social care in England.

sections.source_quote: Schedule 15 makes provision about information standards for health and adult social care in England (under Part 9 of the Health and Social Care Act 2012) and information technology.

sections.heading: Grant of smart meter communication licences
sections.title: Smart Meter Communication Licences
sections.summary: This section refers to Schedule 16, which provides provisions for the grant of smart meter communication licences. It does not directly address data transfers or processing but may impact communications related to smart meters.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes a framework for smart meter communication licences, which may indirectly affect data transfers related to smart meter communications.

requirements.text: Schedule 16 makes provision in connection with the grant of smart meter communication licences.

sections.source_quote: Schedule 16 makes provision in connection with the grant of smart meter communication licences.

sections.heading: Disclosure of information to improve public service delivery to undertakings
sections.title: Amending disclosure rules for public service data sharing
sections.summary: This section amends the Digital Economy Act 2017 to expand the definition of 'undertaking' to include households and charitable entities. It clarifies that disclosures to improve public service delivery can be made to households or undertakings, and specifies that 'charitable purpose' aligns with the Charities Act 2011. The changes apply to both England and Wales and Scotland.
sections.dtia_summary: This section modifies data sharing definitions for public services, requiring explicit clarification of who can receive disclosures and what constitutes charitable purposes. It impacts cross-border data transfers involving public authorities and private entities.

requirements.text: In subsection (9), substitute 'or households' with ', households or undertakings'
In subsection (10), insert 'or assisting of undertakings in connection with any trade, business or charitable purpose'
In this section, 'undertaking' means any person carrying on a trade or business, whether or not with a view to profit
In this section, 'undertaking' means any body or trustees of a trust established for charitable purposes only
In this section, 'charitable purpose' has the same meaning as in the Charities Act 2011

sections.source_quote: Section 35 of the Digital Economy Act 2017 (disclosure of information to improve public service delivery) is amended as follows.

sections.heading: Retention of information by providers of internet services in connection with
sections.title: Retention of child death data by internet services
sections.summary: This section amends the Online Safety Act 2023 to require internet service providers to retain specific child death-related data for one year when a coroner investigates a child's death. It mandates detailed information sharing with OFCOM, including the child's name, birth date, email addresses, and service usage details. Providers must notify OFCOM within a specified timeframe if they retain the data, and OFCOM may extend retention by up to six months under certain conditions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section imposes strict data retention obligations for internet services following child deaths, requiring detailed information sharing with regulators and specific retention periods. It directly impacts data storage and handling for sensitive child-related cases.

requirements.text: OFCOM must require providers to retain information about a child's service usage
Retained information must include the child's name, birth date, email addresses, and service usage details
Providers must notify OFCOM within a specified date of steps taken to retain information
OFCOM may extend retention period by up to six months under specific conditions
Information must be retained for one year from notice date
Providers must specify consequences of non-compliance with retention requirements

sections.source_quote: OFCOM must give a notice to the provider of a service within subsection (E1) requiring the provider to ensure the retention of information relating to the use of the service by the child who has died.

sections.heading: Information for research about online safety matters
sections.title: Research data access for online safety
sections.summary: This section allows the Secretary of State to require internet service providers to share data for independent online safety research. Regulations may specify application procedures, information handling safeguards, and fees, but must exclude data processing that violates data protection laws and legal professional privilege. The Secretary of State must consult relevant bodies before making regulations, and enforcement may include penalties up to those in section 113.
sections.dtia_summary: This section enables regulated services to share data with researchers for online safety purposes, with strict safeguards to prevent data misuse and ensure compliance with data protection laws. It affects data sharing practices for sensitive research activities.

requirements.text: Secretary of State may require providers to share information for online safety research
Regulations may specify application procedures and information handling safeguards
Regulations must not require data processing that contravenes data protection legislation
Regulations must not require information that could be protected by legal professional privilege
Secretary of State must consult OFCOM, Information Commissioner, and other stakeholders
Regulations may impose monetary penalties for non-compliance

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State may by regulations require providers of regulated services to provide information for purposes related to the carrying out of independent research into online safety matters.

sections.heading: Retention of biometric data and recordable offences
sections.title: Biometric data retention and international offences
sections.summary: This section amends the Counter-Terrorism Act 2008 to define 'recordable-equivalent offence' for international cases. It specifies that law enforcement authorities must treat certain foreign convictions as equivalent to domestic convictions for biometric data retention purposes. The amendments apply only to data obtained or retained within three years before the Act's commencement date.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes international equivalence for biometric data retention, requiring law enforcement to treat foreign convictions as domestic for data retention purposes. It impacts cross-border data handling and retention for law enforcement activities.

requirements.text: A 'recordable-equivalent offence' means an offence under foreign law that would constitute a recordable offence in England and Wales
A person is treated as convicted of a foreign offence if a court makes a finding equivalent to not guilty by reason of insanity or under disability
The amendments apply only to biometric data obtained or retained within three years before the Act's commencement

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of section 18A, a person is to be treated as having been convicted of an offence in a country or territory outside England and Wales if, in respect of such an offence, a court exercising jurisdiction under the law of that country or territory has made a finding equivalent to—

sections.heading: Retention of pseudonymised biometric data
sections.title: Retention of pseudonymised biometric data by law enforcement
sections.summary: This section amends the retention rules for pseudonymised biometric data. Law enforcement authorities may retain non-DNA material indefinitely if they obtain it from overseas authorities, it identifies individuals, they pseudonymise it promptly, and continue holding it pseudonymised. Retention ends after 3 years from when they stop holding it pseudonymised, with exceptions for low-risk individuals with no or one conviction.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires pseudonymisation of biometric data before international transfers and sets a 3-year retention period for law enforcement data from overseas sources, impacting cross-border data storage and retention.

requirements.text: Law enforcement may retain non-DNA material indefinitely if obtained from overseas authorities
Material must identify the person to whom it relates
Authority must pseudonymise material promptly after acquisition
Authority must continue holding material in pseudonymised form
Retention period is 3 years from when authority stops holding material pseudonymised
Material may be retained until end of 3-year period for low-risk individuals with no or one conviction

sections.source_quote: Section 18 material which is not a DNA sample may be retained indefinitely by a law enforcement authority if— (a) the authority obtains or acquires the material directly or indirectly from an overseas law enforcement authority, (b) the authority obtains or acquires the material in a form which includes information which identifies the person to whom the material relates, (c) as soon as reasonably practicable after obtaining or acquiring the material, the authority takes the steps necessary for it to hold the material in a pseudonymised form, and (d) having taken those steps, the law enforcement authority continues to hold the material in a pseudonymised form.

sections.heading: Retention of biometric data from INTERPOL
sections.title: Retention of INTERPOL biometric data
sections.summary: This section amends rules for retaining biometric data obtained through INTERPOL. Law enforcement authorities may retain INTERPOL material until notified that the request or notification has been cancelled or withdrawn. The National Central Bureau may retain it until it becomes aware of cancellation. This applies to both pre-commencement and post-commencement material.
sections.dtia_summary: Sets retention period for INTERPOL biometric data based on cancellation notification, affecting cross-border data transfers and international cooperation mechanisms.

requirements.text: Law enforcement may retain INTERPOL material until notified of cancellation or withdrawal
National Central Bureau may retain material until aware of cancellation
Material includes corrections, updates or supplements to original requests

sections.source_quote: The law enforcement authority may retain the material until the National Central Bureau informs the authority that the request or notification has been cancelled or withdrawn.

sections.heading: The eIDAS Regulation
sections.title: eIDAS Regulation reference
sections.summary: This section defines 'the eIDAS Regulation' as Regulation (EU) 2014/2014 on electronic identification and trust services for electronic transactions in the internal market. It specifies the regulation's full title and effective date.
sections.dtia_summary: References the eIDAS Regulation for electronic identification and trust services, which may impact cross-border data transfers and digital services compliance.

requirements.text: The eIDAS Regulation means Regulation (EU) 2014/2014 on electronic identification and trust services for electronic transactions in the internal market

sections.source_quote: In sections 130 to 134, 'the eIDAS Regulation' means Regulation (EU) /2014 of the European Parliament and the Council of 23 July 2014 on electronic identification and trust services for electronic transactions in the internal market.

sections.heading: Recognition of EU conformity assessment bodies
sections.title: Recognition of EU conformity assessment bodies
sections.summary: This section amends the eIDAS Regulation to recognize EU conformity assessment bodies. Bodies are treated as qualified if they meet equivalent EU law standards for trust services, facilitating cross-border electronic services compliance.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows EU conformity assessment bodies to be recognized for cross-border electronic services, enabling international trust services compliance.

requirements.text: A body is treated as a conformity assessment body if it meets equivalent EU law standards for trust services

sections.source_quote: For the purposes of Articles 20(1), 21 and 24(1)(d), a body is to be treated as if it were a conformity assessment body in relation to a description of trust services provider (and trust service) if it is a conformity assessment body in relation to that description of provider (and service) for the purposes of the equivalent EU law.

sections.heading: Removal of recognition of EU standards etc
sections.title: Removal of recognition of EU standards
sections.summary: This section removes recognition of EU standards for qualified trust services. It revokes specific eIDAS Regulation articles related to EU conformity assessment bodies and transitional measures, allowing for removal of EU references in trust services.
sections.dtia_summary: Revokes EU recognition mechanisms for trust services, potentially impacting cross-border electronic signatures and data transfers.

requirements.text: Remove circumstances where something is treated as qualified under eIDAS Regulation
Revoke Article 24A of eIDAS Regulation
Revoke Article 24B of eIDAS Regulation
Revoke Article 51 of eIDAS Regulation

sections.source_quote: amend Article 24A of the eIDAS Regulation (recognition of EU standards etc for qualified trust services) so as to remove circumstances in which something is to be treated as qualified under that Regulation for the purposes of a provision or measure specified in paragraph 1 of that Article

sections.heading: Recognition of overseas trust products
sections.title: Recognition of overseas trust products
sections.summary: This section amends the eIDAS Regulation to recognize overseas trust products. The Secretary of State may regulate that overseas electronic signatures and seals are treated as qualified if they meet reliability standards equivalent to UK-qualified products, with specific requirements for public sector use.
sections.dtia_summary: Allows overseas trust products to be recognized as qualified for cross-border electronic services, with reliability checks and public sector compliance requirements.

requirements.text: Secretary of State may regulate overseas trust products as qualified if reliability is equivalent
Reliability must be at least equivalent to comparable UK-qualified products
Secretary of State must consider relevant laws in other countries when making regulations

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State may by regulations provide that, for the purposes of Articles 25(2), 35(2), 41(2) and 43(2), an overseas trust product of a specified description is to be treated as qualified.

sections.heading: Co-operation between supervisory authority and overseas authorities
sections.title: Co-operation between UK and overseas data authorities
sections.summary: This section amends the eIDAS Regulation to replace references to EU authorities with overseas authorities. It defines 'designated overseas authority' as one designated by regulations made by the Secretary of State. The Secretary of State must consult the supervisory body before making regulations under this section, and regulations may include transitional provisions. These regulations are subject to parliamentary annulment.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes mechanisms for UK data authorities to cooperate with overseas regulators, defining what constitutes an overseas authority and requiring consultation with the supervisory body before implementing regulations. It affects cross-border data transfer compliance by clarifying regulatory oversight frameworks.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State must consult the supervisory body before making regulations under this section
Regulations under this section may include transitional or transitory provision or savings
Regulations under this section are to be made by statutory instrument
A statutory instrument containing regulations under this section is subject to annulment in pursuance of either House of Parliament

sections.source_quote: In paragraph 4, for 'public authority in the EU' substitute 'designated overseas authority'. In paragraph 2, for 'other than in accordance with the data protection legislation' substitute 'if the processing would contravene the data protection legislation (but in determining whether processing would do so, take into account the power conferred by that paragraph').

sections.heading: Time periods: the eIDAS Regulation and the EITSET Regulations
sections.title: Time periods for eIDAS and EITSET regulations
sections.summary: This section amends the eIDAS Regulation and EITSET Regulations to standardize time periods for references to hours, days, months, and years. It specifies that periods in the EITSET Regulations are interpreted according to Article 3 of the Council of 3 June 1971 regulation. The amendments affect monetary penalties by setting a 21-day period for certain notices.
sections.dtia_summary: This section standardizes time periods for regulatory compliance, particularly affecting monetary penalties and notice periods in cross-border data transfer contexts. It ensures consistent interpretation of timeframes across different regulatory frameworks.

requirements.text: References in the eIDAS Regulation to periods expressed in hours, days, months or years are to be interpreted in accordance with Article 3 of Regulation (EEC, Euratom) /71 of the Council of 3 June 1971
References in the EITSET Regulations to periods expressed in days or years are to be interpreted in accordance with Article 3 of Regulation (EEC, Euratom) /71 of the Council of 3 June 1971
In Schedule 1 (monetary penalties), paragraph 4(f) is substituted with 'the period of 21 days beginning when the notice of intent is served'
In Schedule 1 (monetary penalties), paragraph 5 is substituted with 'the period of 21 days beginning when the notice of intent is received'
In Schedule 1 (monetary penalties), paragraph 6 is substituted with 'the period of 21 days beginning when the notice of intent is served'

sections.source_quote: References in these regulations to a period expressed in days or years are to be interpreted in accordance with Article 3 of Regulation (EEC, Euratom) /71 of the Council of 3 June 1971 determining the rules applicable to periods, dates and time limits.

sections.heading: Economic impact assessment
sections.title: Economic impact assessment for copyright and AI
sections.summary: This section requires the Secretary of State to prepare and publish an economic impact assessment within 9 months of the Act's passing. The assessment must cover the economic impact on copyright owners and AI system developers (including individuals, micro businesses, small businesses, and medium-sized businesses) for policy options related to copyright law and AI training using copyright works.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates an economic impact assessment for copyright and AI policy options, which could influence data transfer and processing practices involving AI systems and copyright works. It affects financial institutions through potential impacts on AI development and copyright enforcement.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State must prepare and publish an assessment of the economic impact in the United Kingdom of each of the four policy options described in section B.4 of the Copyright and AI Consultation Paper
The assessment must include economic impact on copyright owners and persons who develop or use AI systems
The assessment must consider the impact on individuals, micro businesses, small businesses, and medium-sized businesses

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State must, before the end of the period of 9 months beginning with the day on which this Act is passed—prepare and publish an assessment of the economic impact in the United Kingdom of each of the four policy options described in section B.4 of the Copyright and AI Consultation Paper

sections.heading: Report on the use of copyright works in the development of AI systems
sections.title: Report on copyright works in AI system development
sections.summary: This section requires the Secretary of State to prepare and publish a report on the use of copyright works in AI system development within 9 months of the Act's passing. The report must consider technical measures to control copyright works, the effect of copyright on data access for AI developers, disclosure of information about copyright work usage, licensing for AI developers, and enforcement mechanisms for copyright restrictions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates a report on copyright works in AI development, which could impact data sharing and processing practices involving AI systems. It affects financial institutions through potential impacts on AI development and copyright enforcement.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State must prepare and publish a report on the use of copyright works in the development of AI systems
The report must consider technical measures and standards to control the use of copyright works to develop AI systems
The report must consider the effect of copyright on access to and use of data by AI developers
The report must consider disclosure of information by AI developers about their use of copyright works
The report must consider licensing for AI developers to do acts restricted by copyright
The report must consider ways of enforcing requirements and restrictions relating to copyright works

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State must, before the end of the period of 9 months beginning with the day on which this Act is passed—prepare and publish a report on the use of copyright works in the development of AI systems

sections.heading: Progress statement
sections.title: Progress statement on economic impact and reports
sections.summary: This section requires the Secretary of State to review the progress on economic impact assessments and reports related to copyright and AI within 9 months of the Act's passing. The review must be completed within two years of the section coming into force, with the outcome published in a report.
sections.dtia_summary: This section mandates a progress statement on economic impact and reports related to copyright and AI, which could influence data transfer and processing practices involving AI systems. It affects financial institutions through potential impacts on AI development and copyright enforcement.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State must review the operation of the economic impact assessment and report progress
The outcome of the review must be published in a report before the end of two years from the section's coming into force

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State must—review the operation of subsection (5), publish the outcome of the review in a report before the end of the period of two years beginning with the day on which this section comes into force

sections.heading: Creating, or requesting the creation of, purported intimate image of adult
sections.title: Purported intimate images: offences and definitions
sections.summary: This section introduces new offences for creating or requesting the creation of purported intimate images of adults. It defines consent, reasonable belief, and the age determination for images. The offences are punishable by imprisonment or fines, with a two-year review period for the operation of the consent defence. The section also includes provisions for treating purported intimate images as used for certain offences in other legislation.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes legal definitions and offences related to purported intimate images, which could impact data privacy and security practices. It affects financial institutions through potential impacts on data handling and user consent mechanisms.

requirements.text: A person commits an offence if they intentionally create or request the creation of a purported intimate image of another person without consent
The Secretary of State must review the operation of the consent defence and publish the outcome within two years
The maximum term for summary offences is six months if committed before the Criminal Justice Act 2003 comes into force, or 51 weeks after that time

sections.source_quote: A person (A) commits an offence if—(a) A intentionally requests the creation of a purported intimate image of another person (B) (either in general or specific terms), (b) B does not consent to A requesting the creation of the purported intimate image, and (c) A does not reasonably believe that B consents.

sections.heading: Power to make consequential amendments
sections.title: Power to make consequential amendments
sections.summary: The Secretary of State can issue regulations that are consequential on any provision of this Act. These regulations may apply to different purposes, include transitional provisions, amend or repeal other legislation, and are subject to specific procedures depending on whether they affect primary legislation or not. The regulations must define 'enactment' and 'primary legislation' to ensure clarity in their application.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State may by regulations make provision that is consequential on any provision made by this Act.
Regulations under this section may make different provision for different purposes.
Regulations under this section may include transitional, transitory or saving provision.
Regulations under this section may amend, repeal or revoke any provision made by an enactment.

The reference in subsection (2)(c) to provision made by an enactment is where the amendment, repeal or revocation is consequential on section 117, 118 or 119(1) or Schedule 14, a reference to provision made by an enactment whenever passed or made (including this Act).

The reference in subsection (2)(c) to provision made by an enactment is in any other case, a reference to provision made by an enactment passed or made before the end of the Session in which this Act is passed.

Regulations under this section made in consequence of section 183A of the Data Protection Act 2018 may amend, repeal or revoke provision which refers to the data protection legislation as they could if the provision referred instead to the main data protection legislation.
Regulations under this section that amend, repeal or revoke primary legislation are subject to the affirmative resolution procedure.
Any other regulations under this section are subject to the negative resolution procedure.

sections.source_quote: 139 Power to make consequential amendments (1) The Secretary of State may by regulations make provision that is consequential on any provision made by this Act.

sections.heading: Regulations
sections.title: Regulations procedure
sections.summary: Regulations under this Act must be made by statutory instrument. If subject to the affirmative resolution procedure, they require approval by both Houses of Parliament. If subject to the negative resolution procedure, they can be annulled by either House of Parliament. Regulations under the negative procedure may be made by regulations under the affirmative procedure.

requirements.text: Regulations under this Act are to be made by statutory instrument.

Where regulations under this Act are subject to 'the affirmative resolution procedure' the regulations may not be made unless a draft of the statutory instrument containing them has been laid before Parliament and approved by a resolution of each House of Parliament.

Where regulations under this Act are subject to 'the negative resolution procedure' the statutory instrument containing the regulations is subject to annulment in pursuance of a resolution of either House of Parliament.

Any provision that may be included in regulations under this Act made by the Secretary of State or the Treasury subject to the negative resolution procedure may be made by regulations subject to the affirmative resolution procedure.

sections.source_quote: 140 Regulations (1) Regulations under this Act are to be made by statutory instrument.

sections.heading: Extent
sections.title: Extent of application
sections.summary: This Act applies to England and Wales, Scotland, and Northern Ireland with specific exceptions. Certain provisions extend only to England and Wales, Northern Ireland, or specific regions like the Channel Islands and British overseas territories. The Act includes mechanisms for extending provisions to these regions through specific powers.

requirements.text: This Act extends to England and Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland, subject to subsections (2) to (5).

In Part 4 (registers of births and deaths)—an amendment or repeal made by Schedule 3 (minor and consequential amendments) has the same extent as the enactment amended or repealed.
In Part 6 (the Information Commission), paragraph 23 of Schedule 12A to the Data Protection Act 2018 (inserted by Schedule 14 to this Act) extends to England and Wales and Northern Ireland only.
In Part 7—section 121 and Schedule 15 (information standards for health and adult social care) extend to England and Wales only.
In Part 7—paragraphs 2, 3 and 5 to 7 of Schedule 16 (grant of smart meter communication licences) extend to England and Wales and Scotland only.
