In Part 7—section 138(4) (amendment of the Armed Forces Act 2006) extends to British overseas territories except Gibraltar.
In Part 7—section 138(5) (amendment of the Serious Crime Act 2007) extends to England and Wales and Northern Ireland only.

sections.source_quote: 141 Extent (1) This Act extends to England and Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland, subject to subsections (2) to (5).

sections.heading: Commencement
sections.title: Commencement of Schedule 1
sections.summary: Schedule 1 of this Act commences on the date specified in the order. This provision ensures that the schedule is activated on a specific date as determined by the relevant authority.

requirements.text: Schedule 1 commences on the date specified in the order.

sections.heading: Transitional, transitory and saving provision

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Appeal procedures for penalty notices
sections.summary: This section outlines procedures for appealing penalty notices. The Tribunal may review factual determinations and consider new evidence not available to the Secretary of State at the time of the notice. The penalty notice remains ineffective until the appeal is determined or withdrawn. If the appeal is successful, the person must comply with the confirmed or varied notice within a specified period or as directed by the Secretary of State.
sections.dtia_summary: This section affects DTIA by specifying appeal timelines and compliance requirements for penalty notices, which may impact data transfer processes if penalties relate to cross-border data handling.

requirements.text: Confirm or quash the decision to give the penalty notice
Confirm or vary any requirement of the penalty notice
Review factual determinations on which the decision is based
Take into account evidence not available to the Secretary of State at notice time
The penalty notice is ineffective until appeal determination or withdrawal
Comply with the confirmed or varied notice within specified period
Comply with the notice as varied within period specified by Secretary of State

sections.source_quote: confirm or quash the decision to give the penalty notice, or confirm or vary any requirement of it.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Conditions for processing personal data in specific public interest scenarios
sections.summary: This section defines specific conditions under which processing personal data is permissible for public interest purposes. It permits processing for public security, emergencies, crime detection, protection of vital interests, safeguarding vulnerable individuals, taxation purposes, and compliance with legal obligations. The conditions require that the processing aligns with specific public interest objectives and meets defined criteria such as reasonable belief in compliance with standards for vulnerable individuals.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes conditions for permissible data processing in public interest scenarios, which may affect cross-border data transfers if the processing involves international entities. It requires specific safeguards for vulnerable individuals and compliance with legal obligations.

requirements.text: Processing is necessary for public security
Processing is necessary for responding to emergencies
Processing is necessary for detecting, investigating or preventing crime
Processing is necessary for apprehending or prosecuting offenders
Processing is necessary for protecting vital interests of data subject or another individual
Processing is necessary for safeguarding vulnerable individuals
Vulnerable individual means aged under 18 or aged 18 or over at risk
At risk means having needs for care and support, experiencing or at risk of neglect or harm
Processing is necessary for taxation purposes
Processing is necessary for complying with legal obligations

sections.source_quote: Schedule 5 – Purpose limitation: processing to be treated as compatible with original purpose

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Amendments to UK GDPR for international data transfers
sections.summary: This section amends the UK GDPR to establish new rules for transferring personal data to third countries or international organizations. It introduces Article 44A requiring approval through regulations, appropriate safeguards, or derogations for specific situations. The data protection test evaluates whether the standard of data protection in the destination country meets the UK's standards, considering legal frameworks, enforcement mechanisms, and international obligations.
sections.dtia_summary: This section creates a framework for international data transfers requiring data protection tests and regulatory approvals, directly impacting DTIA requirements for cross-border transfers. It mandates specific safeguards and ongoing monitoring of data protection standards.

requirements.text: Controller may transfer personal data to third countries only if condition in paragraph 2 is met
Transfer must be approved by regulations under Article 45A
Transfer must comply with other provisions of the Regulation
Secretary of State may approve transfers to third countries through regulations
Data protection test must be met for transfers
Data protection test considers respect for rule of law, human rights, enforcement mechanisms, judicial redress, transfer rules, and international obligations
Secretary of State must monitor developments affecting data protection standards
Secretary of State must amend or revoke regulations if data protection test is no longer met

sections.source_quote: SCHEDULE 7 – Section 85: Transfers of personal data to third countries etc: general processing

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Transfers subject to appropriate safeguards for law enforcement
sections.summary: This section amends Article 47 to require that transfers of personal data to third countries or international organisations for law enforcement purposes must be subject to appropriate safeguards. The Secretary of State may issue regulations specifying standard data protection clauses that meet the data protection test under Article 46. These regulations must be reviewed periodically and follow specific procedural rules including negative resolution procedures. The safeguards must ensure the data protection test is met for law enforcement transfers.
sections.dtia_summary: Transfers for law enforcement must comply with specific safeguards to ensure data protection standards are met. Regulations may specify standard clauses or additional safeguards, requiring periodic review and adherence to procedural rules.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State may by regulations specify standard data protection clauses that secure the data protection test for transfers of personal data generally or for specific types of transfers.
The Secretary of State must keep under review standard data protection clauses specified in regulations.
Regulations under paragraph 1 are subject to the negative resolution procedure.
The Secretary of State may by regulations make provision about further safeguards for law enforcement transfers.
The Secretary of State may only make regulations about further safeguards if they secure the data protection test.
Regulations about further safeguards may include adopting safeguards prepared by another person.
Regulations about further safeguards may require authorisation from the Commissioner.
Regulations under paragraph 4 may only amend Article 46 by adding or varying ways of providing safeguards.

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State may by regulations specify standard data protection clauses which the Secretary of State considers are capable of securing that the data protection test set out in Article 46 is met in relation to transfers of personal data generally or in relation to a type of transfer specified in the regulations.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Conditions for cross-border data transfers under specific security scenarios
sections.summary: This section amends conditions for transferring personal data to third countries by requiring transfers to be made in reliance on section 73(4)(b) for specific security purposes. It mandates that subsequent transfers must include authorisation from the UK authoriser unless the transfer addresses imminent threats to public or national security. The UK authoriser must be notified without delay if transfers occur without such authorisation.
sections.dtia_summary: Requires data transfers to be authorised by the UK authoriser for security-related cross-border transfers, with immediate notification if transfers occur without authorisation. This imposes specific conditions on transfers involving national security threats.

requirements.text: Transfers must be made in reliance on section 73(4)(b) for subsequent transfers
Subsequent transfers must include authorisation from the UK authoriser unless addressing imminent threats to public or national security
UK authoriser must be notified without delay if transfers occur without authorisation
Transfers addressing imminent threats to public or national security require UK authoriser authorisation

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Amendments to data protection legislation definitions and sections
sections.summary: This section amends multiple provisions of the Data Protection Act 2018 by inserting, omitting, or modifying specific clauses. It includes changes to definitions, section wording, and the scope of certain rights, such as data subject access and journalistic practices. The amendments are implemented through statutory instruments and affect the interpretation of key terms like 'data protection legislation' and 'court'.
sections.dtia_summary: These amendments clarify the scope of data protection legislation and journalistic data processing, which may impact how data transfers and processing activities are structured for compliance with international standards.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Information notices: confidentiality and disclosure restrictions
sections.summary: This section modifies information notices to require persons to provide the Commissioner with information for PEC Regulations compliance investigations. It mandates that recipients of notices must not disclose the notice's existence without reasonable excuse, except for disclosures to employees, those with Commissioner permission, or for legal advice purposes. The notices must include confidentiality statements and explanations of disclosure restrictions.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes disclosure restrictions for information notices that affect data transfer across borders by requiring confidentiality and limiting disclosure to specific parties, relevant for assessing cross-border data transfer risks.

requirements.text: Require any person to provide Commissioner with information for PEC Regulations compliance investigations
Require communications providers to provide Commissioner with information about another person's use of electronic services for PEC Regulations compliance
Require any person to provide Commissioner with information for investigating suspected PEC Regulations non-compliance
Recipients of information notices must not disclose notice existence without reasonable excuse
Disclosures to employees, Commissioner-permitted parties, or for legal advice are exempt from disclosure restrictions

sections.source_quote: Section 142 has effect as if—(a) in subsection (1), for paragraphs (a) and (b) there were substituted—"(a) require any person to provide the Commissioner with information or documents that the Commissioner reasonably requires for the purposes of determining whether that person has complied or is complying with the requirements of the PEC Regulations, (b) require a communications provider to provide the Commissioner with information or documents relating to another person's use of an electronic communications network or electronic communications service for the purposes of determining whether that other person has complied or is complying with the requirements of the PEC Regulations, or (c) require any person to provide the Commissioner with information or documents that the Commissioner reasonably requires for the purposes of investigating a suspected failure by another person to comply with the requirements of the PEC Regulations"

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Penalty notices for compliance failures
sections.summary: This section modifies penalty notices to apply to persons who fail to comply with PEC Regulations. It specifies that penalty notices may be issued to officers of bodies if the failure occurred with their consent or neglect. The definition of 'body' and 'officer' is clarified to include corporate structures and specific roles.
sections.dtia_summary: This section defines conditions under which penalty notices can be issued to officers for compliance failures, relevant for assessing risks in cross-border data transfer operations involving third parties.

requirements.text: Penalty notices may be issued to officers of bodies for failures with their consent or neglect
Body means a body corporate or Scottish partnership
Officer includes directors, managers, secretaries, or members of bodies

sections.source_quote: Section 155 has effect as if—(a) in paragraph (a), for "as described in section 149(2), (3), (4), (5) or (5A)" there were substituted "to comply with a requirement of the PEC Regulations"

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Deputy Chair and Non-Executive Member Tenure Rules
sections.summary: This section outlines the tenure rules for deputy chairs and non-executive members of the Commission. Deputy chairs may resign via written notice, cease holding office upon ceasing non-executive membership, and may be removed by the Commission. Non-executive members appointed by the Secretary of State hold office for up to seven years, may resign with written notice, and can be removed by the Secretary of State for serious misconduct, conflicts of interest, non-compliance with procedures, or inability to perform functions. The Secretary of State must publish removal decisions and provide reasons to affected members.
sections.dtia_summary: These tenure rules establish conditions for non-executive member appointments and removals, which may impact data transfer governance if the Commission handles cross-border data processing. The requirement for written notice and specific removal grounds could affect data subject rights and accountability in international contexts.

sections.source_quote: A deputy chair of the Commission may resign that office by giving written notice to the Commission. A deputy chair of the Commission ceases to hold that office on ceasing to be a non-executive member of the Commission. A deputy chair of the Commission may be removed from that office by the Commission.

sections.heading: Short title (cont.)
sections.title: Information standards for IT providers
sections.summary: This section amends information standards procedures to allow adoption of standards prepared by others or international agreements. It requires the Secretary of State to monitor compliance with these standards through notices and potential public censure. The section also establishes accreditation schemes for IT services in healthcare and social care sectors with specific criteria and review processes.
sections.dtia_summary: This section establishes mechanisms for monitoring compliance with information standards across IT providers, relevant to data transfer impact assessments through its oversight of standards implementation and accreditation processes.

requirements.text: The Secretary of State may give written notices to IT providers requiring compliance with information standards
IT providers must comply with information standards within a specified period
The Secretary of State may publish statements of non-compliance with information standards
IT providers must provide evidence of compliance with information standards
The Secretary of State must give IT providers a copy of proposed statements before publication
Regulations may establish accreditation schemes for IT services in healthcare and social care
Accreditation criteria must be set by reference to information standards
Accreditation schemes must publish details of accreditation criteria

sections.source_quote: The Secretary of State may give the provider a written notice which— (a) identifies the standard in question, (b) sets out the Secretary of State's grounds for suspecting that the provider is not complying with the standard, (c) asks the provider to comply with the standard within a period specified in the notice, (d) asks the provider, within a period specified in the notice, to provide evidence to the Secretary of State's satisfaction that the provider is complying with the standard, and (e) if the Secretary of State considers it appropriate, sets out the steps that the Secretary of State considers the provider must take, within a period specified in the notice, in order to comply with the standard.
